你有什么想法？

尤金·沃洛克
|
2026年7月20日 凌晨3:00
下一篇："红袜精神"与宪法理论
尤金·沃洛克是斯坦福大学胡佛研究所的托马斯·M·西贝尔高级研究员，同时担任加州大学洛杉矶分校法学院加里·T·施瓦茨荣誉杰出法学教授和杰出研究教授。当然，他在这里的文章（如同其他博主的观点一样）仅代表个人观点，不代表任何机构。他还共同主持"言论自由不停播"播客。

articles.title: "Red Sox Spirit" and Constitutional Theory
articles.content:
在一篇批评夏威夷最高法院最近根据夏威夷州宪法解释“Aloha精神”的文章中，联合博主乔希·布莱克曼建议，“红袜精神”可能与解释马萨诸塞州宪法相关。这令人惊讶，因为乔希是个纽约球迷，却能看到这一点。但红袜精神确实有关联！尽管我自己以前没看出来，尽管我在马萨诸塞州长大。

红袜精神的核心是保护自由和抵抗暴政，尤其是抵抗自封的棒球“邪恶帝国”。大卫“大帕蒂”奥尔蒂斯——红袜精神最伟大的践行者——最好地诠释了这一点：“这是我们该死的城市，没人能决定我们的自由。”

在2022年Bruen案中，美国最高法院采用的“历史与传统”宪法解释方法下，马萨诸塞州宪法应按照这一原则解释！它确实是该州历史和传统的关键要素。

抵抗暴政是马萨诸塞州宪法传统的核心，体现在最好的方面。美国独立革命始于马萨诸塞州，起因是英国帝国暴政及其对自由和财产权的诸多侵犯。几年后，1783年，马萨诸塞州成为第一个用宪法废除奴隶制的州，当时州最高法院裁定这违反了宪法中要求所有人“自由和平等”的条款（佛蒙特州1777年已废除奴隶制，但当时还不是一个州）。

今天，该州应当好好运用红袜精神来限制租金管制和排他性区划，这些政策威胁宪法财产权，并造成住房短缺，使许多人无法享受该州提供的自由。而且，正如乔希在他的文章中建议的，携带武器权利的保护也是红袜精神的核心（即使州最高法院另有裁定）。毕竟，独立革命之所以爆发，是因为英国人试图没收列克星敦和康科德民兵持有的武器，而这些私人武器使人民能够有效抵抗英国人并最终击败他们。

将红袜精神正确应用于州宪法法并不总是明确的。但是，当有疑问时，问问大帕蒂会怎么做！然后再为自由出击。

稍微严肃一点，我认为“历史与传统”是解释联邦宪法的一个存在严重缺陷的方法。传统的“原始含义”原意主义通常更好。但对联邦宪法最好的方法并不总是适用于州宪法。州法院可能有理由使用与联邦法院不同的解释方法。而且，正如从自由派最高法院大法官威廉·布伦南到著名保守派法官杰弗里·萨顿等法学家所争论的，各州常常能够且应该使用其宪法比最高法院解释联邦宪法更广泛地保护权利。例如，许多州在对最高法院在Kelo诉新伦敦市案（2005年）中糟糕的财产权裁决做出回应时就这么做了。乔希援引红袜精神在这点上是对的！

我认为乔希是对的，担保条款——保证每个州拥有“共和制政府”——具有可诉性，不应被视为不可诉的“政治问题”。政治问题 doctrine总体上是一团糟。没有充分理由认为法院在确定什么是共和政府方面不如解释宪法中其他许多略显模糊的条款（如必要和适当条款或第四修正案要求搜查必须“合理”）的能力更强。

与乔希不同，我对夏威夷的枪支政策是否违反该条款持怀疑态度。担保条款的原始含义可能只施加相当基本的要求，也许不过如此：立法和行政分支由选举产生的官员控制，没有世袭君主制和贵族。毕竟，独立时代的大多数人显然认为奴隶制和极为有限的选举权与该条款是相容的。一些夏威夷枪支限制确实违反了第二修正案，包括最高法院最近否决的那些。各州可以比联邦宪法更多地保护权利，但不能更少。

联邦对州的武装胁迫——比如最近在“地铁突袭行动”中对明尼苏达州采取的那些——也可能与担保条款不相容。如果有什么能破坏共和政府，那就是以枪口威胁夺取州权力。但也许我们不需要依赖担保条款，考虑到同样的结果可以根据目前具有可诉性的第十修正案得出。

与乔希不同，我认为联邦宪法中“入侵”的含义具有可诉性。更多内容请看我关于该主题的文章，我在其中解释为什么执行正确的定义（军事攻击，而非非法移民或毒品走私）对保护权力分立、防止州政府和联邦政府滥用公民自由至关重要。

总之，乔希可能（尽管是无意中）用“红袜精神”触及了某些东西！

更新：本文已做了一些小的修订，包括纠正了我最初对乔希·布莱克曼关于“入侵”可诉性立场的误解。

“红袜精神”与宪法理论这篇文章首次出现在Reason.com上。
articles.summary:
In the course of a post criticizing the Hawaii Supreme Court's recent "Aloha Spirit" decision interpreting the Hawaii state constitution, co-blogger Josh Blackman suggests that "Red Sox Spirit" might be relevant to interpreting the Massachusetts constitution. It's surprising that it took a New York fan like Josh to see it. But Red Sox Spirit is indeed relevant! Even though I myself didn't see it, previously, despite growing up in Massachusetts.

Red Sox Spirit is all about protecting liberty and resisting tyranny, especially that of the self-proclaimed Evil Empire of Baseball. David "Big Papi" Ortiz - the greatest-ever exponent of Red Sox Spirit summarized it best: "This is our f-ing city and nobody gonna dictate our freedom":

Under the "history and tradition" approach to constitutional interpretation applied by the US Supreme Court in the 2022 Bruen Second Amendment case, the Massachusetts Constitution should be interpreted in accordance with that principle! It's certainly a crucial element of the state's history and tradition.

Resisting tyranny is central to the Massachusetts constitutional tradition, at its best. The American Revolution began in Massachusetts, in response to British imperial tyranny, and its many violations of liberty and property rights. A few years later, in 1783, Massachusetts became the first state to use its constitution to abolish slavery, when the state supreme court held that it violated the constitutional provision mandating that all men are "free and equal" (Vermont had abolished slavery in 1777, but it was not yet a state at the time).

Today, the state would do well to apply the Red Sox Spirit of constitutional law to curb rent control and exclusionary zoning, which threaten constitutional property rights, and create housing shortages that make it impossible for many to enjoy the liberty the state offers. And, as Josh suggests in his post, protection of the right to bear arms is also central to Red Sox Spirit (even if the state's supreme court has ruled otherwise). After all, the Revolution began because the British sought to confiscate arms kept by the Lexington and Concord minutemen, and those privately owned weapons enabled the people to effectively resist the British and eventually defeat them.

The proper application of Red Sox Spirit to state constitutional law isn't always clear. But, when it doubt, ask what Big Papi would do! And then strike another blow for freedom.

On a slightly more serious note, I think "history and tradition" is a badly flawed approach to interpreting the federal Constitution. Conventional "original meaning" originalism is generally better. But what is best for federal constitutional isn't always the right approach for state constitutions. State courts might be justified in using different interpretive methods than federal courts. And, as jurists ranging from liberal Supreme Court Justice William Brennan to prominent conservative Judge Jeffrey Sutton have argued, states often can and should use their constitutions to protect rights more broadly than the Supreme Court's interpretation of the federal Constitution. For example, many did that in response to the Supreme Court's badly flawed property rights ruling in Kelo v. City of New London (2005). Josh's invocation of Red Sox Spirit is on the right track, there!

I think Josh is right that the Guarantee Clause, which guarantees a "republican form of government" to each state, is justiciable, and should not be considered a nonjusticiable "political question."  The political question doctrine is generally a mess. And there is no good reason to think courts are somehow less capable of determining what a republican government is than they are of interpreting any number of other somewhat imprecise provisions of the Constitution, such as the Necessary and Proper Clause, or the Fourth Amendment's requirement that searches must be "reasonable."

Unlike Josh, I am skeptical that Hawaii's gun control policies violate that Clause. The original meaning of the Guarantee Clause probably imposes only fairly minimal requirements, perhaps not much more than that the legislative and executive branches be controlled by elected officials, and that there be no hereditary monarchy and aristocracy. After all, most in the Founding era apparently thought slavery and an extremely limited franchise were compatible with the Clause. Some Hawaii gun restrictions do violate the Second Amendment, however, including those recently invalidated by the Supreme Court. States can protect rights more than the federal Constitution does, but not less.

And it may be that federal armed coercion of states - like that recently undertaken in Minnesota during "Operation Metro Surge" - is also incompatible with the Guarantee Clause. If anything undermines republican government, it's usurpation of state authority at literal gunpoint. But maybe we don't need to rely on the Guarantee Clause here, given that the same result can be reached under the currently justiciable Tenth Amendment.

Unlike Josh, I think that the meaning of "invasion" in the federal Constitution is justiciable. For more, see my article on that subject, where I explain why enforcing the correct definition (a military attack, not illegal migration or drug smuggling) is crucial to protecting the separation of powers and preventing both state and federal governments from abusing civil liberties.

In sum, Josh may (however unintentionally) be onto something with the "Red Sox Spirit"!

UPDATE: I have made a few minor revisions to this post, including correcting my initial misunderstanding about Josh Blackman's position on the justiciability of "invasion."

The post "Red Sox Spirit" and Constitutional Theory appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: "Academics Spent Years Lending Their Institutional Credibility to Various Political Projects"
articles.content:
现在他们在声誉上已经破产了。如果不修复损害，不说服人们继续写支票，许多机构最终也会在财务上破产。

Megan McArdle（《华盛顿邮报》）的明智之言。同一专栏的更多内容：

学术界需要向公众推销其项目的价值。他们必须说服人们，“大觉醒”时期的激进宣言是一个可怕的错误，现在已经得到纠正，他们正在追求真理，而非政治议程。学术界有许多人确实理解这一点，包括委托撰写《人文与人文社会科学学术现状报告》的范德堡大学和圣路易斯华盛顿大学的管理人员。

但许多人仍然懵懂无知。即使他们隐约意识到出了严重问题，也不明白需要做什么才能解决这个问题。他们所谓的解决方案是傲慢地告诉美国人他们错了：错了——学术界让政治感染了其工作，错了——改革是必要的，错了——只有两种性别，男性和女性。

这篇帖子《学术界花费多年将其机构信誉借给各种政治项目》首发于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
Now they are reputationally bankrupt. If they don't repair the damage, and convince people to keep writing the checks, many institutions will eventually be financially bankrupt as well.

Wise words from Megan McArdle (Washington Post). A bit more from the same column:

Academics need to sell the public on the value of their project. They must convince people that the radical pronouncements of the Great Awokening were a terrible mistake, now corrected, and that they are pursuing truth, not political agendas. Many people in academia do understand this, including the administrators who commissioned {the "Report on the State of Scholarship in the Humanities and the Humanistic Social Sciences," commissioned by Vanderbilt and Washington University in St. Louis}.

But many others are still clueless. Even if they grasp, dimly, that something is seriously amiss, they do not understand what it will take to fix the problem. Their idea of a solution is to imperiously inform Americans they are wrong: wrong that academia has let politics infect its work, wrong that reform is needed, and wrong that there are two sexes, male and female.

The post "Academics Spent Years Lending Their Institutional Credibility to Various Political Projects" appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Calling Cartels 'Narcoterrorists' Turns Drug Enforcement Into War Making
articles.content:
在大多数国家，帮派战争在暗处进行。警方收集尸体，检察官召开新闻发布会，任何不看新闻的人都可以假装什么都没发生。而在里约热内卢的贫民窟，暴力活动看起来更像是传统战争：领土易手，武装哨兵在山丘上巡逻，穿着人字拖的青少年公开携带军用级步枪。“在里约热内卢，有三个主要派系，”法比奥·塞拉（Fabio Serra）说，他更为人熟知的名字是萨加特（Sagat B）。“我们有很多枪，但主要是为了争夺地盘而互相争斗。如果警察来了，我们只有在别无选择的情况下才会与他们战斗——交火是为了掩护我们撤退。”

萨加特曾经管理过一个毒贩团队。“我比其他人都聪明，非常擅长处理数字，所以我负责毒品销售的物流工作，也负责采购武器和弹药，”他解释道。当时，他隶属于巴西两大最强大的犯罪组织：里约的红军指挥部（Red Command）和总部位于圣保罗的第一资本指挥部（PCC）。经历了三次入狱、累计12年之后，萨加特告别了犯罪生涯。他现在是一位作家、播客主持人、理发店老板和反犯罪活动人士。但他的前同伙已成为一场更大运动的目标：特朗普政府试图将毒贩重新定位为恐怖组织，将毒品走私定性为战争。自重返白宫以来，唐纳德·特朗普总统升级了针对“毒品恐怖分子”的运动，将毒品走私视为“武装攻击”，模糊了刑事执法与战争之间的界限。他的政府将外国毒品 cartel 和其他犯罪团伙描述为“非国家武装团体”或“非法人战斗人员”——这是布什时代引入的一个术语，旨在剥夺疑似恐怖分子根据日内瓦公约享有的保护。去年8月，特朗普签署了一份秘密指令，授权对毒品恐怖分子进行军事打击。到9月，美军开始轰炸据称在加勒比海的毒品船只。12月，政府随后发布行政命令，将芬太尼列为大规模杀伤性武器，声称它可以被“武器化，用于有组织敌对势力的大规模集中恐怖袭击”。

“我想我们就是要杀死那些把毒品带进我们国家的人，”特朗普说。毒品恐怖分子的标签不断扩大。今年5月，国务院将PCC和红军指挥部列入恐怖主义黑名单，将其指定为外国恐怖组织。但这对于毒品管制有什么成效呢？这个标签之所以重要，是因为它改变了美国政府可用的工具。毒品走私犯通常是刑事嫌疑人。恐怖分子可以被当作军事目标处理。从执法到战争制造的这一转变，是特朗普新毒品战略的核心举措，尽管将 cartel 视为恐怖分子的想法已经酝酿多年。特朗普在第一个任期期间曾考虑过这一举措，此前2019年 cartel 枪手在墨西哥北部杀害了九名来自摩门教社区的美国妇孺。随着美国过量用药危机加剧，这一想法持续存在——21世纪初每年死亡人数超过10万人。已故参议员林赛·格雷厄姆（R-S.C.）甚至将墨西哥 cartel 与ISIS和基地组织等恐怖组织进行比较。

PCC的例子说明了为什么这个标签诱人——以及为什么它可能具有误导性。该组织诞生于1992年的卡兰迪鲁监狱骚乱，当时军事警察冲进设施并杀害了111名囚犯。PCC成为一个犯罪集团，但它也充当囚犯维权团体和家庭支持网络的角色。它的反建制言论吸引了被疏远的年轻囚犯，尤其是那些遭受过警察暴力的人，以及在毒品战争中不成比例地被监禁的非洲裔巴西人。“当我第一次入狱时，我担心自己会无缘无故地受到虐待，”萨加特回忆道。相反，他说，他收到了一本规则手册。囚犯被禁止互相虐待，争议由一个委员会裁决，只要狱警有尊严地对待囚犯，就必须尊重他们。“这就是为什么监狱里没有发生坏事，”他说。“我们团结一致对抗当局。”

但PCC的规则并没有完全消除暴力，萨加特说，当监狱当局使用酷刑时，“局势失控，夺走了囚犯和狱警的生命。”

虽然PCC有一种类似意识形态的东西——囚犯权利、相互保护以及对滥用国家权力的抵抗——但它也 capable of great violence. 2006年5月，该组织使南美洲最大的城市圣保罗陷入瘫痪，对警察局、公共汽车、银行和公共建筑发动协调攻击，同时全州范围内爆发了监狱骚乱。在四天的时间里，39名警察和监狱警卫以及4名平民丧生。“他们有时将恐怖作为工具，”帕拉伊巴联邦大学副教授马科斯·阿兰·费雷拉（Marcos Alan Ferreira）说。但他补充道，“他们更喜欢使用其他工具，因为暴力会招致（国家更强烈的回应）。”

圣保罗袭击事件发生后的几周里，警察暗杀小队据称处决了500多人，其中一些人是这座城市最贫困社区的无辜旁观者。尽管如此，费雷拉认为，PCC的核心目的并非政治改革。“他们的意识形态是经济利益，”他说，“主要是毒品交易，但近年来，在亚马逊地区涉及非法采矿或木材开采等环境犯罪。”

这种获利动机帮助PCC成为巴西最大的帮派，拥有超过10万名成员和关联者，并与意大利的“光荣会” mafia 成为跨大西洋可卡因贸易的主要参与者。“这与伊斯兰恐怖主义或任何其他将恐怖作为实现政治目标工具的团体相去甚远，”费雷拉说。它在里约的对应组织红军指挥部有着更加明确的意识形态起源。20世纪70年代末，左翼激进分子与普通罪犯一起被囚禁在巴西版阿卡特拉兹的格兰德岛上。激进分子带来了革命理论；罪犯带来了抢劫、走私和街头暴力的实用知识。但到80年代，可卡因贸易扩大，贫民窟成为理想的露天市场，红军指挥部已经从为革命而抢劫变成了一个 conventional gang。“恐怖主义这个词并不真正适用，因为即使这些派系极其暴力，也从未有过任何认真的意图来推翻政府建立新政权，”萨加特说。但一旦华盛顿贴上这个标签，问题就不再是它是否适用，而是这个标签允许美国政府做什么。“他们是否与基地组织或伊斯兰国有一些相似之处并不重要，”乔治梅森大学教授、《Carteles, Inc.》一书的作者瓜达卢佩·科雷亚-卡布雷拉（Guadalupe Correa-Cabrera）说。“根据美国法律，它们现在是外国恐怖组织，这真正改变了美国在西半球边界之外的整体角色……现在你可以直接使用军事手段来追捕这些被提名的恐怖组织，这是从执法问题向军事问题的转变。”

恐怖主义标签将毒品战争变成了更接近 actual war 的东西。曾经的运动通过逮捕、引渡、查封和制裁推进，现在可以通过军事打击、情报行动和区域安全联盟来推进。最明显的证据是美国政府公布的模糊 footage，显示据称运毒的船只在委内瑞拉加勒比海岸被炸毁。迄今为止，已有221人被杀，其中包括一些据称被“双击”的人——在第一次袭击中幸存后再次遭到袭击——或在被俘试图投降时遭到袭击。美国政府坚持其目标是基于情报挑选的毒品走私者，但没有公开出示他们有罪的证据。鉴于乔治·W·布什和巴拉克·奥巴马总统时期的法外无人机 campaigns 记录，有理由持怀疑态度。在普通的毒品案件中，政府的说法可以在法庭上得到检验。而在这里，目标往往在这种情况发生之前就已经死亡。空袭损害了毒品战争所依赖的合作。毒品走私本质上是一种跨国业务：古柯叶不能在内布拉斯加种植。任何认真的打击走私的努力都需要国际合作。相反，出于担心被卷入战争罪，英国、荷兰和加拿大等国据报道已停止与美国共享情报。船只袭击只是更广泛军事行动中最引人注目的部分。中央情报局也在帮助追踪墨西哥的毒品头目，而美国正在厄瓜多尔领导与军队的联合行动。3月，厄瓜多尔总统丹尼尔·诺博亚、萨尔瓦多总统纳伊布·布克尔、阿根廷总统哈维尔·米莱和其他右倾领导人加入了盾牌行动（Shield of the Americas），这是一个美国领导的打击犯罪团伙的联盟。趋势很明确：在整个西半球，毒品战争正在变得越来越军事化。巴西目前不是美国空袭的目标。但这显示了这种军事化思维在逻辑上可能导致的结果。10月，里约军警发动了“围堵行动”（Operation Containment），这是一次 paramilitary invasion 进入佩尼亚和阿莱芒贫民窟的行动，旨在逮捕一名红军指挥部头目及其副手。这次突袭是巴西历史上最致命的一次，造成117至132人死亡。嫌疑人撤退到附近的树林，据报道他们直接陷入了警察伏击。警察向任何移动的东西开枪。呼应特朗普的言论，里约州长克劳迪奥·卡斯特罗（Cláudio Castro）将这次行动描述为打击“毒品恐怖主义”的重大成功。

但头目逃脱了，居民报告说第二天就看到帮派成员在街头巡逻。这次行动可能提高了州长的支持率，但对打破帮派对贫民窟的控制几乎没有作用。恐怖主义 designation 也给一个老工具带来了新的 reach：金融制裁。任何与这些组织做生意的人，无论是否知情，都可能面临银行账户被冻结、企业被制裁、美国签证被撤销。而且由于PCC现在涉足从公共交通到医疗保健的一切领域，这些处罚可能远远超出帮派成员的范围，延伸到 ordinary economic life。“不要玩弄这个国家的主权，”巴西总统路易斯·伊纳西奥·卢拉·达席尔瓦（Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva）在美国将犯罪组织标记为恐怖分子后警告说。“不要玩弄我们的民主。”

尽管战争恐怖主义的 rhetoric 不断，但特朗普的禁毒战略几乎没有产生成功的证据。可卡因仍然容易获得且价格可负担。价格基本稳定在每克60到100美元左右。与此同时，美国海关和边境保护局（CBP）截获的可卡因数量实际上有所增加，从袭击开始前的八个月超过43000磅增加到9月到4月期间的近48000磅。这并不一定意味着CBP正在获胜。国土安全部（DHS）估计，只有约3%的可卡因在边境被查获。更多的查获可能只是反映了更高的 traffic 或路线的改变。幸运的是，过量用药死亡人数一直在大幅下降，CBP查获的芬太尼数量也在减少（可能表明现在跨境运送的这种强效阿片类药物减少了）。但这两个积极趋势都始于乔·拜登总统任期内，远在特朗普开始击沉疑似走私者之前。原因主要是其他因素——包括中国的供应链中断和过量用药逆转药物纳洛酮的更广泛供应——而不是对毒品分子的发动战争。即使针对毒品走私者的军事打击有效，它们似乎也指向了错误的方向。从委内瑞拉和巴西运送的 much of the cocaine 目的地是大西洋彼岸，而不是美国。因此，攻击这些路线对美国毒品市场影响甚微也就不足为奇了。“关于犯罪组织的反应，我不认为对他们来说会太困难，因为重要的是要记住，对于PCC和红军指挥部来说，在国际上，欧洲和西非市场比美国市场更重要，”费雷拉说。“也许他们会避免前往北美，但总的来说，我不认为他们太担心。”

糟糕的结果并不是怀疑这一战略的唯一理由。



毒品-恐怖主义框架似乎也会因政治便利而变通。今年12月,特朗普赦免了洪都拉斯前总统胡安·奥兰多·埃尔南德斯,这位被定罪的可卡因走私者来自与特朗普支持的总统候选人相同的右翼政党。然而,本届政府却对委内瑞拉领导人尼古拉斯·马杜罗就类似指控采取远为激进的行动。同一个毒品战争框架,既可以为某个政治盟友软化适用,也可以针对敌人加强力度。PCC和红色指挥的恐怖主义定性也是在巴西总统候选人、 前总统雅伊尔·博索纳罗之子弗拉维奥·博索纳罗的游说之后做出的。与此同时,博索纳罗家族长期面临与里约黑手党式准军事组织联系的审查。这些主要由在职和退休警察组成的武装团体从红色指挥手中夺取了里约大部分地区的控制权,从事着与贫民窟毒贩相同的许多犯罪活动,但拥有制度性保护和政治人脉。值得注意的是,这些准军事组织并未出现在美国国务院的黑名单上。恐怖主义定性的选择性使用揭示了毒品-恐怖主义框架的更深层问题。它将犯罪组织视为意识形态敌人来对待,而更好的理解是将其视为非法市场的供应商。其结果并非更好的毒品管控,而是更广泛的战争授权许可。暴力并非来自恐怖主义,而是来自禁令:一个被宣布非法的市场,利润极高,因此值得为之杀戮。
articles.summary:
In most countries, gang wars unfold in the shadows. Police collect the bodies, prosecutors hold press conferences, and anyone who avoids the news can pretend nothing is happening. In Rio de Janeiro's favelas, the violence can look more like conventional war: territory changes hands, armed lookouts patrol the hills, and teenagers in flip-flops carry military-grade rifles in the open. 

"In Rio de Janeiro, there are three main factions [gangs]," says Fabio Serra, better known as Sagat B. "We have a lot of guns but mainly to fight each other over territory. If the police come, we'll only fight them if there's no other choice—the exchange of gunfire is to cover our escape."

Sagat once managed a crew of drug dealers. "I was more intelligent than the others and very good at dealing with numbers, so I handled the logistics of selling the drugs as well as purchasing weapons and ammunition," he explains. At the time, he was affiliated with Brazil's two most powerful criminal organizations: Rio's Red Command and the Sao Paulo–based First Capital Command (PCC). 

After three prison terms totaling 12 years, Sagat left crime behind. He is now an author, podcaster, barbershop owner and anti-crime activist. But his former associates have become targets in a much larger campaign: the Trump administration's efforts to recast drug gangs as terrorist organizations and drug trafficking as war.  

Since returning to office, President Donald Trump has escalated his campaign against "narcoterrorists," treating drug smuggling as an "armed attack" and blurring the line between criminal enforcement and war. His administration has described foreign drug cartels and other criminal gangs as "non-state armed groups" or "unlawful combatants," a term introduced during the Bush era to deny suspected terrorists protections afforded under the Geneva Conventions.

In August of last year, Trump signed a secret directive authorizing military strikes on narco-terrorists. By September, U.S. forces were bombing alleged drug boats in the Caribbean. In December, the administration followed with an executive order classifying fentanyl as a weapon of mass destruction, claiming that it could be "weaponized for concentrated, large-scale terror attacks by organized adversaries."

"I think we're just gonna kill people that are bringing drugs into our country," Trump said.

The narco-terrorist label keeps expanding. In May, the State Department added the PCC and Red Command to its terrorism blacklist, designating them as foreign terrorist organizations. But what has that accomplished for drug control?

Why the Terrorist Label Changes What the U.S. Military Can Do

The label matters because it changes the tools available to the U.S. government. A drug trafficker is normally a criminal suspect. A terrorist can be treated as a military target. That shift—from law enforcement to war making—is the central move in Trump's new drug strategy, though the idea of treating cartels as terrorists has been gaining ground for years. 

Trump considered the move during his first term after cartel gunmen killed nine American women and children from a Mormon community in northern Mexico in 2019. The idea persisted as the overdose crisis worsened in the U.S., with annual deaths surpassing 100,000 in the early 2020s. The late Sen. Lindsey Graham (R–S.C.) even compared the Mexican cartels to terrorist groups like ISIS and Al Qaeda.

The PCC shows why the label is tempting—and why it can be misleading. 

The group was born after the Carandiru prison riot in 1992, which ended when military police stormed the facility and killed 111 inmates. The PCC became a crime syndicate, but it also functions as a prisoners' advocacy group and family support network. Its antiestablishment rhetoric appeals to alienated young prisoners, especially those who have experienced police brutality, and to Afro-Brazilians, who are disproportionately imprisoned in the war on drugs.

"When I first arrived in prison, I was afraid I'd be abused for no reason," Sagat remembers. Instead, he says, he was given a rulebook. Prisoners were forbidden from abusing one another, disputes were judged by a commission, and guards were to be treated with respect so long as they treated prisoners with dignity. "That's why bad things didn't happen in the prison," he says. "We were all united against the authorities."

But PCC rules did not eliminate violence altogether, and when prison authorities used torture, Sagat says, "the situation gets out of control, claiming the lives of both inmates and guards."

While the PCC has something like an ideology—prisoners' rights, mutual protection, and resistance to abusive state power—it's also capable of great violence. In May 2006, the group paralyzed Sao Paulo—South America's biggest metropolis—with coordinated attacks on police stations, buses, banks, and public buildings, while prison riots broke out across the state. Over the course of four days, 39 police officers and prison guards, and four civilians, lost their lives.

"They use terror as a tool sometimes," says Marcos Alan Ferreira, an associate professor at the Federal University of Paraíba. But, he adds, "they prefer to use other tools because violence invites [a stronger response from the state]."

In the weeks after the Sao Paulo attacks, police death squads allegedly executed more than 500 people, some of them innocent bystanders from the city's poorest neighborhoods.

Still, Ferreira argues, the PCC's core purpose is not political reform. "Their ideology is economic gain," he says, "mainly dealing in drugs but in recent years, environmental crimes in the Amazon like illegal mining or timber exploitation."

That profit motive has helped make the PCC Brazil's largest gang, with more than 100,000 members and associates, and a major player in the trans-Atlantic cocaine trade together with Italy's 'Ndrangheta mafia. "This is far from Islamic terrorism or any other group that uses terror as a tool to reach political objectives," Ferreira says. 

Its counterpart in Rio, the Red Command, had more explicitly ideological origins. In the late 1970s, left-wing militants were imprisoned alongside ordinary criminals on Ilha Grande, Brazil's version of Alcatraz. The militants brought revolutionary theory; the criminals brought practical knowledge of robbery, smuggling, and street violence. But by the 1980s, the cocaine trade had expanded, the favelas had become ideal open-air markets, and the Red Command had become less about robbing for the revolution and more of a conventional gang.

"The word terrorism doesn't really fit, because even if the factions are extremely violent, there's never been any serious intention to overthrow the government for a new regime," says Sagat.

But once Washington applies the label, the question is no longer just whether it fits. It's what the label allows the U.S. government to do.  

"It doesn't matter if they have some similarities or not to Al Qaeda or the Islamic State," says Guadalupe Correa-Cabrera, a professor at George Mason University and author of the book Carteles, Inc. "They are now foreign terrorist organizations for U.S. law, and that really changes the whole role of the United States outside its frontiers within the Western Hemisphere….Now you can use the military directly to go after these nominated terrorist organizations, and this is a transformation from a law enforcement problem towards a military issue."

How the Drug War Went From Arrests to Airstrikes

The terrorism label turns the drug war into something closer to an actual war. A campaign that once moved through arrests, extraditions, seizures, and sanctions can now move through military strikes, intelligence operations, and regional security alliances. 

The clearest evidence is the U.S. government's grainy footage of alleged drug-smuggling boats being blown out of the water off the Caribbean coast of Venezuela. So far, 221 people have been killed, including some who were allegedly "double-tapped"—struck again after surviving the first hit—or attacked while attempting to surrender.

The U.S. government insists its targets are drug smugglers chosen based on intelligence, but it has not publicly presented evidence of their guilt. Given the record of U.S. intelligence during the extrajudicial drone campaigns of Presidents George W. Bush and Barack Obama, there is reason to be skeptical. In an ordinary drug case, the government's claims could be tested in court. Here, the targets are often dead before that can happen.

The strikes have damaged the cooperation the drug war depends on. Drug trafficking is, by definition, a transnational business: Coca leaves can't be grown in Nebraska. Any serious effort to combat trafficking requires international cooperation. Instead, fearful of being implicated in war crimes, Britain, the Netherlands and Canada are among those who've reportedly stopped sharing intelligence with the U.S. 

The boat strikes are only the most visible part of a broader military campaign. The CIA is also helping track down narco leaders in Mexico, while the U.S. is leading joint operations with the military in Ecuador. In March, Ecuadorian President Daniel Noboa, Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele, Argentine President Javier Milei, and other right-leaning leaders joined the Shield of the Americas, a U.S.-led alliance against criminal gangs. The trend is clear: Across the Western Hemisphere, the drug war is becoming increasingly militarized.

Brazil is not being targeted by U.S. airstrikes. But it shows what that militarized thinking can look like when led to its logical conclusion. In October, Rio's military police launched Operation Containment, a paramilitary invasion of the Penha and Alemão favelas meant to arrest a Red Command boss and his lieutenants. The raid was the deadliest in Brazilian history, leaving between 117 and 132 dead. Suspects retreated into nearby woods, where they reportedly ran straight into a police ambush. Officers fired at anything that moved. Echoing Trump's rhetoric, Rio Gov. Cláudio Castro described the operation as a great success against "narcoterrorism."

But the boss escaped, and residents reported seeing gang members patrolling the streets the very next day. The operation may have boosted the governor's approval ratings, but it did little to break the gang's grip on the favelas. 

The terrorist designation also gives an old tool new reach: financial sanctions. Anyone who does business with these groups, knowingly or unknowingly, can have bank accounts frozen, businesses sanctioned, and U.S. visas revoked. And since the PCC now has interests in everything from public transport to healthcare, those penalties could reach far beyond gang members and into ordinary economic life.

"Do not play games with the sovereignty of this country," Brazil's President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva warned in response to the U.S. labeling criminal groups as terrorists. "Do not play games with our democracy." 

Cocaine Prices, Seizures, and Overdose Deaths Since the Strikes Began

For all the war-on-terror rhetoric, Trump's counternarcotics strategy has produced little evidence of success. Cocaine remains accessible and affordable. Prices have stayed roughly stable, at about $60 to $100 a gram. 

Meanwhile, the volume of cocaine intercepted by U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) actually increased, from more than 43,000 pounds in the eight months before the strikes began to nearly 48,000 between September and April. That doesn't necessarily mean the CBP is winning. The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) estimates that only about 3 percent of cocaine is seized at the border. Higher seizures might simply reflect higher traffic or shifting routes. 

Fortunately, overdose deaths have been falling dramatically, as has the quantity of fentanyl seized by the CBP (likely indicating that less of the potent opioid is now being sent across the border). But both of those positive trends began during Joe Biden's presidency, well before Trump began sinking suspected smugglers. The causes are largely other factors—including supply chain disruptions in China and wider availability of the overdose-reversing drug naloxone—rather than waging war against narcos.

Even if military strikes against drug smugglers were effective, they appear to be aimed in the wrong direction. Much of the cocaine shipped from Venezuela and Brazil is headed across the Atlantic, not to the United States. It is therefore unsurprising that attacking these routes has done little to affect the U.S. drug markets. 

"Regarding the reaction of criminal organizations, I don't think things will be too difficult for them because it's important to remember that for both the PCC and Red Command, internationally, the European and West African markets are more important than the U.S.," says Ferreira. "Maybe they will avoid any travel to North America, but in general, I don't think they are too concerned."

Why Trump Pardoned One Trafficker and Targeted Maduro for the Same Crime

The poor results are not the only reason to doubt the strategy. The narco-terrorism framework also appears to bend around political convenience. 

In December, Trump pardoned former Honduran President Juan Orlando Hernández, a convicted cocaine trafficker from the same right-wing party as the presidential candidate that Trump was backing. Yet the administration pursued far more aggressive action against Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro over similar allegations. The same drug-war framework that could be softened for one political ally could be intensified against an enemy. 

The PCC and Red Command's terrorist designation also came after lobbying from Flávio Bolsonaro, a presidential candidate and the son of former Brazilian President Jair Bolsonaro, a Trump ally. The Bolsonaro family, meanwhile, has long faced scrutiny over its links to Rio's milícias—mafia-like paramilitary gangs made up largely of off-duty and former police officers. These groups have wrested control of large parts of Rio from the Red Command, and engage in many of the same criminal activities as favela drug dealers, but with institutional protection and political connections. 

Notably, the milícias are not on the State Department's blacklist.

The selective use of terrorist designations points to a deeper problem with the narco-terrorism framework. It treats criminal organizations as ideological enemies when they are better understood as suppliers in an illegal market. The result is not better drug control, but a broader permission slip for war making. The violence follows not from terrorism, but from prohibition: a market made illegal, extremely profitable, and therefore worth killing over.

The post Calling Cartels 'Narcoterrorists' Turns Drug Enforcement Into War Making appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Today in Supreme Court History: July 19, 1949
articles.content:
1949年7月19日：墨菲大法官去世。

本文最初发表于Reason.com：今日最高法院史：1949年7月19日。
articles.summary:
7/19/1949: Justice Frank Murphy dies.

The post Today in Supreme Court History: July 19, 1949 appeared first on Reason.com.
